subject: Physics
topic: Uncategorized
text: Displacement current is the current, which arises due to time rate of change of electric flux ( {tex}\phi{/tex} ) in some part of the electric circuit. Mathematically: I D = {tex}\frac {\epsilon_o \boldsymbol{d} \theta_B}{dt}{/tex} When a capacitor is charged or a charged capacitor is allowed to discharge, the electric flux between the plates of the capacitor changes with time and it gives rise to displacement current between the plates. The conduction and displacement currents are entirely different from each other. However, displacement current produces magnetic field in the same manner, as the conduction current does.
text_html:
Displacement current is the current, which arises due to time rate of change of electric flux ({tex}\phi{/tex}) in some part of the electric circuit.
Mathematically: ID = {tex}\frac {\epsilon_o \boldsymbol{d} \theta_B}{dt}{/tex}
When a capacitor is charged or a charged capacitor is allowed to discharge, the electric flux between the plates of the capacitor changes with time and it gives rise to displacement current between the plates.
The conduction and displacement currents are entirely different from each other. However, displacement current produces magnetic field in the same manner, as the conduction current does.

subject: Physics
topic: Uncategorized
text: The displacement current is always equal to the conduction current. Modified Ampere circuital law states that the line integral of magnetic field B over a closed path is equal to {tex}\mu _0{/tex} , times the sum of the conduction current (I) and the displacement current (I) threading the closed path. Mathematically- {tex}\oint {\vec{B}} \cdot d \vec{l}=\mu_o\left(I_C+ \frac {\epsilon_0 d \theta_B}{ d t}\right){/tex} It is also known as Ampere-Maxwell’s circuital law. Following four equations, which describe the laws of electromagnetism, are called Maxwell’s equations {tex}\oint \vec{E} \cdot d \vec S=\frac q{ \epsilon_o}{/tex} (Gauss's law in electrostatics) {tex}\oint \vec{B} . d \vec S=0{/tex} (Gauss’s law in magnetism) {tex}\oint \vec{E} \cdot d \vec l{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-d \phi_B }{ d t}{/tex} (Faraday’s law of electromagnetic induction) {tex}\oint \vec{E} \cdot d \vec l =\mu_0 I{/tex} (Ampere-Maxwell’s circuital law) Maxwell’s equations are the mathematical formulation of Gauss’s law in electrostatics, Gauss’ law in magnetism, Faraday’s law of electromagnetic induction and Ampere’s circuital law.
text_html:
The displacement current is always equal to the conduction current.
Modified Ampere circuital law states that the line integral of magnetic field B over a closed path is equal to {tex}\mu _0{/tex}, times the sum of the conduction current (I) and the displacement current (I) threading the closed path.
Mathematically- {tex}\oint {\vec{B}} \cdot d \vec{l}=\mu_o\left(I_C+ \frac {\epsilon_0 d \theta_B}{ d t}\right){/tex}
It is also known as Ampere-Maxwell’s circuital law.
Following four equations, which describe the laws of electromagnetism, are called Maxwell’s equations
{tex}\oint \vec{E} \cdot d \vec S=\frac q{ \epsilon_o}{/tex} (Gauss's law in electrostatics)
{tex}\oint \vec{B} . d \vec S=0{/tex} (Gauss’s law in magnetism)
{tex}\oint \vec{E} \cdot d \vec l{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-d \phi_B }{ d t}{/tex} (Faraday’s law of electromagnetic induction)
{tex}\oint \vec{E} \cdot d \vec l =\mu_0 I{/tex} (Ampere-Maxwell’s circuital law)
Maxwell’s equations are the mathematical formulation of Gauss’s law in electrostatics, Gauss’ law in magnetism, Faraday’s law of electromagnetic induction and Ampere’s circuital law.

subject: Physics
topic: Displacement Current
text: Maxwell's Insight on Changing Electric Fields An electrical current produces a magnetic field, and according to Maxwell, a changing electric field also results in a magnetic field. This insight is crucial for understanding electromagnetic waves.
text_html:
Maxwell's Insight on Changing Electric Fields
An electrical current produces a magnetic field, and according to Maxwell, a changing electric field also results in a magnetic field. This insight is crucial for understanding electromagnetic waves.

topic: Displacement Current
text: The Charging of a Capacitor The charging process of a capacitor showcases the influence of a changing electric field. By using Ampere's circuital law, we can study the magnetic fields produced outside the capacitor.
text_html:
The Charging of a Capacitor
The charging process of a capacitor showcases the influence of a changing electric field. By using Ampere's circuital law, we can study the magnetic fields produced outside the capacitor.

topic: Displacement Current

text_html:
Ampere's Circuital Law Application
For a plane circular loop outside a parallel plate capacitor, the magnetic field calculated using Ampere's circuital law is consistent with its symmetrical alignment.

text_html:

A contradiction arises in calculating the magnetic field by Ampere's circuital law for different surfaces, suggesting there may be a missing factor in the law itself.

Electric flux through a given surface, derived using Gauss's law, shows the relationship between the field strength and the charge on the capacitor plates.

subject: Physics

text_html:
Introduction of Displacement Current
Maxwell introduced the concept of displacement current, arising from a changing electric field, as a critical missing term in Ampere's circuital law.

subject: Physics
topic: Displacement Current
text: Displacement Current Effects Displacement current results in magnetic fields even in the absence of conduction currents, demonstrating its significant physical influence.

Displacement current results in magnetic fields even in the absence of conduction currents, demonstrating its significant physical influence.

text: Existence of Electromagnetic Waves Electromagnetic waves result from the symmetrical electric and magnetic fields, as established by Faraday's Law and the newly formulated Ampere-Maxwell Law.
text_html:
Existence of Electromagnetic Waves
Electromagnetic waves result from the symmetrical electric and magnetic fields, as established by Faraday's Law and the newly formulated Ampere-Maxwell Law.

topic: Electromagnetic Waves with thier Characteristics and Transverse Nature
text: propagation, constitute electromagnetic waves . Mathematically- {tex}\frac {E_0}{B_0}{/tex} = c The energy in electromagnetic waves is divided equally between the electric and magnetic field vectors. The electric vector of an electromagnetic wave is responsible for its optical effect. For this reason, the electric vector is also called light vector. The energy transported by electromagnetic waves is given by U = hν = {tex}\frac {hc}{\lambda}{/tex} where U is energy transported by electromagnetic waves in a given time and c is speed of electromagnetic waves in free space. The momentum transported by electromagnetic waves is given by p = {tex}\frac { 𝑼}{𝒄} = \frac {𝒉𝝂}{ 𝒄} = \frac {𝒉}{ \lambda}{/tex} The intensity of electromagnetic waves i.e. energy crossing per second per unit area of a surface normally is given by I = {tex}\frac {𝟏}{ 𝟐 \in_0 𝑬_0 2}{/tex}
text_html:
propagation, constitute electromagnetic waves.
Mathematically- {tex}\frac {E_0}{B_0}{/tex} = c

  • The energy in electromagnetic waves is divided equally between the electric and magnetic field vectors.
  • The electric vector of an electromagnetic wave is responsible for its optical effect. For this reason, the electric vector is also called light vector.
  • The energy transported by electromagnetic waves is given by U = hν = {tex}\frac {hc}{\lambda}{/tex} where U is energy transported by electromagnetic waves in a given time and c is speed of electromagnetic waves in free space.
  • The momentum transported by electromagnetic waves is given by p = {tex}\frac { 𝑼}{𝒄} = \frac {𝒉𝝂}{ 𝒄} = \frac {𝒉}{ \lambda}{/tex} The intensity of electromagnetic waves i.e. energy crossing per second per unit area of a surface normally is given by I = {tex}\frac {𝟏}{ 𝟐 \in_0 𝑬_0 2}{/tex}

subject: Physics
topic: Electromagnetic Waves with thier Characteristics and Transverse Nature
text: **Electromagnetic Wave Sources** Stationary charges generate static fields. Charges in uniform motion generate static magnetic fields. **Key Point:** Accelerated charges emit electromagnetic waves. This is a crucial result from Maxwell's theory. **Conceptual Insight:** An oscillating charge generates an alternating electric field, causing a magnetic field to oscillate, sustaining a wave.
text_html:
**Electromagnetic Wave Sources**
Stationary charges generate static fields.
Charges in uniform motion generate static magnetic fields.

**Key Point:** Accelerated charges emit electromagnetic waves. This is a crucial result from Maxwell's theory.

**Conceptual Insight:**
An oscillating charge generates an alternating electric field, causing a magnetic field to oscillate, sustaining a wave.

subject: Physics
topic: Electromagnetic Waves with thier Characteristics and Transverse Nature
text: **Challenges in Testing Electromagnetic Waves** Technology has limits. It's tough to set AC circuits with frequencies matching visible light (e.g., yellow light). Yellow light frequency is $$ 6 \times 10^{14} \text{Hz} $$. Max electronic circuit frequency is $$ 10^{11} \text{Hz} $$. **Outcome:** Emergent experimentation initially in low-frequency radio waves.
text_html:
**Challenges in Testing Electromagnetic Waves**
Technology has limits. It's tough to set AC circuits with frequencies matching visible light (e.g., yellow light).
Yellow light frequency is $$ 6 \times 10^{14} \text{Hz} $$.
Max electronic circuit frequency is $$ 10^{11} \text{Hz} $$.

**Outcome:** Emergent experimentation initially in low-frequency radio waves.

subject: Physics
topic: Electromagnetic Waves with thier Characteristics and Transverse Nature
text: **Significant Experiments & Discoveries** Heinrich Hertz confirmed Maxwell's theory through experiments (1887). Jagdish Chandra Bose produced shorter wavelength EM waves (25 mm to 5 mm) in Kolkata. Guglielmo Marconi transmitted EM waves over longer distances, paving communication.
text_html:
**Significant Experiments & Discoveries**
Heinrich Hertz confirmed Maxwell's theory through experiments (1887).
Jagdish Chandra Bose produced shorter wavelength EM waves (25 mm to 5 mm) in Kolkata.
Guglielmo Marconi transmitted EM waves over longer distances, paving communication.

subject: Physics
topic: Electromagnetic Waves with thier Characteristics and Transverse Nature
text: **Nature of Electromagnetic Waves** Electric and magnetic fields within waves: Are perpendicular to each other. Are perpendicular to the wave's direction. The electric fields toggle in one plane and magnetic in another while propagating.
text_html:
**Nature of Electromagnetic Waves**
Electric and magnetic fields within waves:
  • Are perpendicular to each other.
  • Are perpendicular to the wave's direction.
The electric fields toggle in one plane and magnetic in another while propagating.

subject: Physics
topic: Electromagnetic Waves with thier Characteristics and Transverse Nature
text: **Mathematical Description** Electric field representation: {tex} E_x = E_0 \sin (kz - \omega t) {/tex} Magnetic field representation: {tex} B_y = B_0 \sin (kz - \omega t) {/tex} Here, {tex} k = \frac{2 \pi}{\lambda} {/tex} (wave number). {tex} \omega = c k {/tex} (wave speed equation).
text_html:
**Mathematical Description**
Electric field representation: {tex} E_x = E_0 \sin (kz - \omega t) {/tex}
Magnetic field representation: {tex} B_y = B_0 \sin (kz - \omega t) {/tex}
Here,

  • {tex} k = \frac{2 \pi}{\lambda} {/tex} (wave number).
  • {tex} \omega = c k {/tex} (wave speed equation).

subject: Physics
topic: Electromagnetic Waves with thier Characteristics and Transverse Nature
text: **Properties of Waves** Analysis shows electric and magnetic fields related as: {tex} B_0 = \left( E_0 / c \right) {/tex} Electromagnetic waves are oscillations in fields, persisting naturally even without a material medium.
text_html:
**Properties of Waves**
Analysis shows electric and magnetic fields related as:
{tex} B_0 = \left( E_0 / c \right) {/tex}
Electromagnetic waves are oscillations in fields, persisting naturally even without a material medium.

subject: Physics
topic: Electromagnetic Waves with thier Characteristics and Transverse Nature
text: **Effects of Material Media** Electromagnetic waves (like light) pass through media like glass. Maximum speed of light in a material depends on its permittivity {tex} \varepsilon {/tex} and magnetic permeability {tex} \mu {/tex} : {tex} v = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}} {/tex} },
text_html:
**Effects of Material Media**
Electromagnetic waves (like light) pass through media like glass.
Maximum speed of light in a material depends on its permittivity {tex} \varepsilon {/tex} and magnetic permeability {tex} \mu {/tex}:
{tex} v = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}} {/tex}},

subject: Physics
topic: Electromagnetic Waves with thier Characteristics and Transverse Nature
text: **Wave Velocity in Vacuum** Speed of EM waves in vacuum is constant and crucial for standards: {tex} 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s} {/tex} This speed applies irrespective of wave's wavelength and is used as a metric defining length.
text_html:
**Wave Velocity in Vacuum**
Speed of EM waves in vacuum is constant and crucial for standards: {tex} 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s} {/tex}
This speed applies irrespective of wave's wavelength and is used as a metric defining length.

subject: Physics
topic: Electromagnetic Waves with thier Characteristics and Transverse Nature
text: **Applications in Technology** Electromagnetic waves are integral in energy transmission. Uses include: Radio and TV signal broadcasting. Natural light energy transfer (e.g., sunlight enabling life on Earth).
text_html:
**Applications in Technology**
Electromagnetic waves are integral in energy transmission.
Uses include:
  • Radio and TV signal broadcasting.
  • Natural light energy transfer (e.g., sunlight enabling life on Earth).

subject: Physics
topic: Electromagnetic Waves with thier Characteristics and Transverse Nature
text: **Summary of Electromagnetic Wave Concepts** Accelerated charges are origins for electromagnetic waves. Waves exhibit perpendicular electric and magnetic fields that sustain across various frequencies. Their technological significance relies on energy transfer abilities, propagation in vacuum, and through different media.
text_html:
**Summary of Electromagnetic Wave Concepts**
Accelerated charges are origins for electromagnetic waves.
Waves exhibit perpendicular electric and magnetic fields that sustain across various frequencies.
Their technological significance relies on energy transfer abilities, propagation in vacuum, and through different media.

subject: Physics
topic: Electromagnetic Spectrum including elementary facts about their uses
text: propagation, constitute electromagnetic waves . The direction of propagation of an electromagnetic wave is given by the cross product of electric field and magnetic field vectors The electromagnetic waves are transverse in nature. *The velocity of electromagnetic waves in free space is given by c = {tex}\frac {1}{\sqrt {\mu_0 \epsilon_0}}{/tex} = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 108 m/s In a material medium, velocity of electromagnetic waves is given by v = {tex}\frac {1}{\sqrt {\mu {\epsilon}}}{/tex} The ratio of the amplitudes of electric and magnetic fields is constant and it is equal to velocity of the electromagnetic waves in free space.
text_html:
propagation, constitute electromagnetic waves.

  • The direction of propagation of an electromagnetic wave is given by the cross product of electric field and magnetic field vectors
  • The electromagnetic waves are transverse in nature. *The velocity of electromagnetic waves in free space is given by c = {tex}\frac {1}{\sqrt {\mu_0 \epsilon_0}}{/tex} = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 108 m/s
  • In a material medium, velocity of electromagnetic waves is given by v = {tex}\frac {1}{\sqrt {\mu {\epsilon}}}{/tex}
  • The ratio of the amplitudes of electric and magnetic fields is constant and it is equal to velocity of the electromagnetic waves in free space.

topic: Electromagnetic Spectrum including elementary facts about their uses
text: Electromagnetic wave spectrum is shown below
text_html: Electromagnetic wave spectrum is shown below

subject: Physics
topic: Electromagnetic Spectrum including elementary facts about their uses
text: Uses of Electromagnetic Spectrum {tex}\gamma{/tex} -rays are highly penetrating; they can penetrate thick iron blocks. Due to high energy, they are used to initiate some nuclear reactions. {tex}\gamma{/tex} -rays are produced in nuclear reactions. In medicine, they are used to destroy cancer cells. X-rays are used in medical diagnostics to detect fractures in bones, tuberculosis of lungs, presence of stone in gallbladder and kidney. They are used in engineering to check flaws in bridges. In physics X-rays are used to study crystal structure. Ultraviolet rays provide vitamin D. These are harmful for skin and eyes. They are used to sterilise drinking water and surgical instruments. They are used to detect invisible writing, forged documents, finger prints in forensic lab and to preserve food items. Infrared rays are produced by hot bodies and molecules. These waves are used for long distance photography and for therapeutic purposes.
text_html:
Uses of Electromagnetic Spectrum

  • {tex}\gamma{/tex}-rays are highly penetrating; they can penetrate thick iron blocks. Due to high energy, they are used to initiate some nuclear reactions. {tex}\gamma{/tex}-rays are produced in nuclear reactions. In medicine, they are used to destroy cancer cells.
  • X-rays are used in medical diagnostics to detect fractures in bones, tuberculosis of lungs, presence of stone in gallbladder and kidney. They are used in engineering to check flaws in bridges. In physics X-rays are used to study crystal structure.
  • Ultraviolet rays provide vitamin D. These are harmful for skin and eyes. They are used to sterilise drinking water and surgical instruments. They are used to detect invisible writing, forged documents, finger prints in forensic lab and to preserve food items.
  • Infrared rays are produced by hot bodies and molecules. These waves are used for long distance photography and for therapeutic purposes.

subject: Physics
topic: Electromagnetic Spectrum including elementary facts about their uses
text: Uses of Electromagnetic Spectrum Radio waves are used for broadcasting programmes to distant places. According to frequency range, they are divided into following groups Medium frequency band or medium waves 0·3 to 3 MHz Short waves or short frequency band 3 MHz - 30 MHz Very high frequency (VHF) band 30 MHz to 300 MHz Ultrahigh frequency (UHF) band 300 MHz to 3000 MHz Microwaves are produced by special vacuum tubes, namely; klystrons, magnetrons and gunn diodes. Their frequency range is 3 GHz to 300 GHz. They are used in RADAR systems for aircraft navigation and microwave used in homes.
text_html:
Uses of Electromagnetic Spectrum
Radio waves are used for broadcasting programmes to distant places. According to frequency range, they are divided into following groups
Medium frequency band or medium waves 0·3 to 3 MHz
Short waves or short frequency band 3 MHz - 30 MHz
Very high frequency (VHF) band 30 MHz to 300 MHz
Ultrahigh frequency (UHF) band 300 MHz to 3000 MHz
Microwaves are produced by special vacuum tubes, namely; klystrons, magnetrons and gunn diodes. Their frequency range is 3 GHz to 300 GHz. They are used in RADAR systems for aircraft navigation and microwave used in homes.

subject: Physics
topic: Electromagnetic Spectrum including elementary facts about their uses
text: Electromagnetic Waves Overview - Initially, only visible light waves were known as electromagnetic waves. - Later discoveries included UV, infrared, X-rays, and gamma rays.
text_html:
Electromagnetic Waves Overview
- Initially, only visible light waves were known as electromagnetic waves.
- Later discoveries included UV, infrared, X-rays, and gamma rays.

subject: Physics
topic: Electromagnetic Spectrum including elementary facts about their uses
text: Electromagnetic Spectrum - The spectrum categorizes EM waves by frequency. - There's a continuous range without sharp divisions. - Classification is based on wave production and detection.
text_html:
Electromagnetic Spectrum
- The spectrum categorizes EM waves by frequency.
- There's a continuous range without sharp divisions.
- Classification is based on wave production and detection.

subject: Physics
topic: Electromagnetic Spectrum including elementary facts about their uses
text: Radio Waves - Produced by charge acceleration in wires. - Used in radio and TV communications. - Frequencies: 500 kHz to 1000 MHz.
text_html:
Radio Waves
- Produced by charge acceleration in wires.
- Used in radio and TV communications.
- Frequencies: 500 kHz to 1000 MHz.

subject: Physics
topic: Electromagnetic Spectrum including elementary facts about their uses
text: Microwaves - Short radio waves with GHz frequencies. - Produced by klystrons, magnetrons, and Gunn diodes. - Common in radar systems and microwave ovens.
text_html:
Microwaves
- Short radio waves with GHz frequencies.
- Produced by klystrons, magnetrons, and Gunn diodes.
- Common in radar systems and microwave ovens.

subject: Physics
topic: Electromagnetic Spectrum including elementary facts about their uses
text: Infrared Waves - Produced by hot bodies; adjacent to visible spectrum. - Absorbed by molecules like water, causing heating. - Used in therapy, earth observation, and household electronics.
text_html:
Infrared Waves
- Produced by hot bodies; adjacent to visible spectrum.
- Absorbed by molecules like water, causing heating.
- Used in therapy, earth observation, and household electronics.

subject: Physics
topic: Electromagnetic Spectrum including elementary facts about their uses
text: Visible Rays - Registered by the human eye. - Wavelengths: 700-400 nm range. - Provides visual information about surroundings.
text_html:
Visible Rays
- Registered by the human eye.
- Wavelengths: 700-400 nm range.
- Provides visual information about surroundings.

subject: Physics
topic: Electromagnetic Spectrum including elementary facts about their uses
text: Ultraviolet Rays - Produced by lamps and hot bodies; sun is a major source. - Absorbed by the ozone layer, with potential harmful effects. - Uses: tanning, LASIK surgery, water purification.
text_html:
Ultraviolet Rays
- Produced by lamps and hot bodies; sun is a major source.
- Absorbed by the ozone layer, with potential harmful effects.
- Uses: tanning, LASIK surgery, water purification.

subject: Physics
topic: Electromagnetic Spectrum including elementary facts about their uses
text: X-Rays - Located beyond UV rays in the spectrum. - Produced by high-energy electron bombardment. - Utilized in medicine for diagnostics and cancer treatment.
text_html:
X-Rays
- Located beyond UV rays in the spectrum.
- Produced by high-energy electron bombardment.
- Utilized in medicine for diagnostics and cancer treatment.

subject: Physics
topic: Electromagnetic Spectrum including elementary facts about their uses
text: Gamma Rays - Highest frequency in the spectrum; shortest wavelengths. - Emanate from nuclear reactions and radioactive decay. - Key application: destroying cancer cells.
text_html:
Gamma Rays
- Highest frequency in the spectrum; shortest wavelengths.
- Emanate from nuclear reactions and radioactive decay.
- Key application: destroying cancer cells.

subject: Physics
topic: Electromagnetic Spectrum including elementary facts about their uses
text: Summary of Electromagnetic Waves Radio: >0.1 m | Charges | Aerial reception Microwave: 0.1 m-1 mm | Vacuum tubes | Diodes Infrared: 1 mm-700 nm | Molecular vibration | Thermopiles Visible: 700-400 nm | Electron energy transitions | Eyes UV: 400 nm-1 nm | Atomic electron transitions | Photocells X-rays: 1 nm-10^{-3} nm | X-ray tubes | Films Gamma:
text_html:
Summary of Electromagnetic Waves
  • Radio: >0.1 m | Charges | Aerial reception
  • Microwave: 0.1 m-1 mm | Vacuum tubes | Diodes
  • Infrared: 1 mm-700 nm | Molecular vibration | Thermopiles
  • Visible: 700-400 nm | Electron energy transitions | Eyes
  • UV: 400 nm-1 nm | Atomic electron transitions | Photocells
  • X-rays: 1 nm-10^{-3} nm | X-ray tubes | Films
  • Gamma: <10^{-3} nm | Radioactive decay | Ionization

subject: Physics
topic: Introduction
text: Magnetic Effect of Electric Current An electric current produces a magnetic field around it. Two current-carrying wires exert magnetic forces on each other due to their magnetic fields.
text_html:
Magnetic Effect of Electric Current
An electric current produces a magnetic field around it. Two current-carrying wires exert magnetic forces on each other due to their magnetic fields.

subject: Physics
topic: Introduction
text: Time-Varying Magnetic and Electric Fields A magnetic field changing with time gives rise to an electric field. The opposite is also true, as argued by James Clerk Maxwell.
text_html:
Time-Varying Magnetic and Electric Fields
A magnetic field changing with time gives rise to an electric field. The opposite is also true, as argued by James Clerk Maxwell.

subject: Physics
topic: Introduction
text: Maxwell's Insights Maxwell suggested that a time-varying electric field also generates a magnetic field. He introduced the concept of displacement current.
text_html:
Maxwell's Insights
Maxwell suggested that a time-varying electric field also generates a magnetic field. He introduced the concept of displacement current.

subject: Physics
topic: Introduction
text: Ampere's Circuital Law and Displacement Current Displacement current was introduced by Maxwell to resolve the inconsistency in Ampere's Circuital Law when applied to time-varying currents in capacitors.
text_html:
Ampere's Circuital Law and Displacement Current
Displacement current was introduced by Maxwell to resolve the inconsistency in Ampere's Circuital Law when applied to time-varying currents in capacitors.

subject: Physics
topic: Introduction
text: Maxwell's Equations Maxwell's equations describe how electric and magnetic fields are generated and altered by each other and by charges and currents.
text_html:
Maxwell's Equations
Maxwell's equations describe how electric and magnetic fields are generated and altered by each other and by charges and currents.

subject: Physics
topic: Introduction
text: Electromagnetic Waves Electromagnetic waves are coupled time-varying electric and magnetic fields that propagate through space.
text_html:
Electromagnetic Waves
Electromagnetic waves are coupled time-varying electric and magnetic fields that propagate through space.

subject: Physics
topic: Introduction
text: Speed of Electromagnetic Waves Predicted by Maxwell's equations, their speed is close to the speed of light, leading to the conclusion that light is an electromagnetic wave.
text_html:
Speed of Electromagnetic Waves
Predicted by Maxwell's equations, their speed is close to the speed of light, leading to the conclusion that light is an electromagnetic wave.

subject: Physics
topic: Introduction
text: Unification of Electricity, Magnetism, and Light Maxwell's work unified electric and magnetic phenomena with light. This was experimentally confirmed by Hertz in 1885.
text_html:
Unification of Electricity, Magnetism, and Light
Maxwell's work unified electric and magnetic phenomena with light. This was experimentally confirmed by Hertz in 1885.

subject: Physics
topic: Introduction
text: Technological Advancements Hertz's demonstration of electromagnetic waves led to technological innovations in communication by Marconi and others.
text_html:
Technological Advancements
Hertz's demonstration of electromagnetic waves led to technological innovations in communication by Marconi and others.

subject: Physics
topic: Introduction
text: Electromagnetic Spectrum The spectrum ranges from {tex}3{/tex} rays, with very short wavelengths {tex} (∼10^{-12}m), to long radio waves (∼10^6 m).{/tex}
text_html:
Electromagnetic Spectrum
The spectrum ranges from {tex}3{/tex} rays, with very short wavelengths {tex} (∼10^{-12}m), to long radio waves (∼10^6 m).{/tex}
